title: Modern Observability Stack
description: Modern observability concepts for SAP engineers. Covers the three pillars (metrics, logs, traces), Prometheus/Grafana, OpenTelemetry, distributed tracing, SLI/SLO monitoring, alerting best practices, and how this differs from SAP Solution Manager and CCMS monitoring.

description.value:
SAP Solution Manager is the default monitoring tool in every SAP landscape. It is centralized, ITIL-based, and focuses on system availability and business process monitoring. It tells you whether the AS ABAP is running, whether the database is connected, and whether background jobs completed. This is system monitoring — it answers the question: Is the system UP?

FAANG observability is fundamentally different. It is distributed, engineer-owned, and focuses on service behavior and user experience. It answers the question: Is the system BEHAVING correctly? A system can be UP (all application servers running, database connected) but BEHAVING badly (p95 latency of 10 seconds, 5% error rate on the checkout API, background jobs timing out). Observability assumes that complex distributed systems will fail in unexpected ways, and the goal is to provide enough instrumentation to debug any unexpected behavior without having to predict it in advance.

Key Differences: SAP SolMan monitors technical components (application servers, databases, RFC connections). Observability monitors service-level indicators (latency, error rate, throughput, saturation). SAP uses a centralized operations team (Basis) to react to SolMan alerts. FAANG uses a distributed SRE model where every engineer is responsible for the observability of their service. SAP's monitoring is configuration-heavy (you define what to monitor in advance). Observability is instrumentation-heavy (you emit telemetry data and explore it at query time). SAP tells you if the system is UP; observability tells you how the system is BEHAVING.

sections.title: The Three Pillars — Metrics, Logs, Traces

description.value:
The three pillars of observability are metrics, logs, and traces. Every FAANG service must emit all three. SAP has equivalents for two but lacks the third entirely, which is the single biggest gap between SAP monitoring and modern observability.

Metrics are numerical measurements collected at regular intervals. Common metrics include request latency (p50, p95, p99), error rate (percentage of requests that fail), throughput (requests per second), and saturation (CPU usage, memory pressure, queue depth). Metrics are lightweight, cheap to collect, and enable dashboards and alerting. SAP's equivalents: ST04 (database performance), STADN (runtime analysis), ST03N (workload analysis). These are metrics, but they are system-level, not service-level. SAP can tell you the average dialog response time; it cannot tell you the p95 latency of the purchase order creation flow.

Logs are detailed, timestamped records of discrete events. Logs are high-cardinality and high-volume, making them expensive to store and query without a search-index platform. SAP's equivalents: SLG1 (application log), SM21 (system log), security audit log. These are logs, but they are not structured, not correlated, and not easily searchable across systems. Every SAP system has its own log files scattered across different directories and formats.

Traces represent the end-to-end flow of a single request as it traverses multiple services, databases, and queues. Each unit of work is a span with a trace ID, parent span ID, start time, duration, and status. Traces are what SAP completely lacks. SAP has no mechanism to trace a Fiori request from the browser through the BTP Gateway, OData service, ABAP function module, RFC call, and HANA query. If the checkout flow is slow, SAP cannot tell you which component is responsible. FAANG engineers solve this with distributed tracing tools like Jaeger, Zipkin, and Grafana Tempo.

sections.title: Prometheus and Grafana — The FAANG Standard

Prometheus and Grafana are the de facto standard for metrics and visualization at FAANG companies. Prometheus is a time-series database that collects metrics via a pull model — it scrapes HTTP endpoints exposed by services and exporters. Every FAANG service exposes a /metrics endpoint with latency histograms, request counters, and error rate gauges. Prometheus stores these as time series with labels (e.g., http_requests_total{method="POST", status="500"}) and supports a powerful query language called PromQL for ad-hoc analysis. Grafana connects to Prometheus (and other data sources like Loki for logs, Tempo for traces) to build interactive dashboards.

SAP Comparison: The closest SAP equivalent to Prometheus is transaction ST03N (workload analysis), which shows dialog, RFC, and update response times aggregated by system. But ST03N is a static report with limited filtering, no time-series storage, and no query language. You cannot ask ST03N: 'Show me the p95 response time of OData service XYZ between 2pm and 3pm yesterday, grouped by client.' With PromQL, that query is: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{service="odata_xyz"}[5m])) by (le)). SAP Solution Manager dashboards are like static Grafana dashboards — they show pre-defined views. Grafana lets you explore data on the fly with no predefined queries. The difference is the difference between a printed map and Google Maps.

OpenTelemetry (OTel) is the industry standard for generating, collecting, and exporting telemetry data (metrics, logs, and traces) in a vendor-neutral format. It is the successor to OpenTracing and OpenCensus, and is now the CNCF standard adopted by all major cloud providers and observability vendors. OTel provides SDKs for every major programming language and a collector component that receives OTel data, processes it (sampling, filtering, batching), and exports it to any backend (Prometheus, Jaeger, Datadog, New Relic, etc.).

OTel unifies the three pillars under a single instrumentation library. Instead of using separate libraries for metrics (Prometheus client), logs (structured logging), and traces (OpenTracing), you use the OTel SDK for all three. The same span that captures a trace can also emit metrics and attach log events. This unified approach eliminates the cost of managing three separate instrumentation pipelines and enables correlation across pillars — you can navigate from a high-latency metric to the trace that caused it, then to the logs of that specific span.

SAP Context: SAP has no equivalent to OpenTelemetry. SAP's protocols are proprietary — RFC, IDoc, BAPI, ALE — and telemetry data stays within the SAP ecosystem. There is no standard way to export ABAP metrics to Prometheus or ABAP traces to Jaeger. Some third-party solutions exist (SAP Cloud ALM, SAP Focused Run) but they are SAP-specific, not OTel-compatible. This means SAP engineers working on hybrid landscapes (SAP on-premise + cloud services) must either build custom OTel bridges or accept that SAP components are black boxes in their observability stack. The community effort to add OTel to ABAP is in its early stages, but SAP BTP is increasingly adopting OTel for cloud services.

Alerting in the FAANG world is built on top of Prometheus alerting rules evaluated by the Alertmanager. Alertmanager handles deduplication, grouping, silencing, and routing to notification channels (PagerDuty, Opsgenie, Slack, email). The key principle is that alerts should be actionable — every alert must require a human response and have a corresponding runbook.

A Prometheus alerting rule consists of: (1) a PromQL expression that triggers the alert when it evaluates to true, (2) a duration (how long the condition must hold before firing), (3) severity (critical, warning, info), (4) labels for routing (team, service, severity), and (5) annotations (summary, description, runbook URL). The evaluation frequency determines how quickly an alert fires — typically every 30 seconds to 1 minute for critical metrics.

SAP Comparison: SAP Solution Manager has alerting rules based on thresholds (e.g., CPU > 90%, dialog response time > 2 seconds). But SolMan alerting is rule-based and not as flexible as PromQL. You cannot define complex conditions (e.g., p95 latency > 1s AND error rate > 2%, sustained for 5 minutes, excluding planned maintenance windows). SolMan alerts are also difficult to route dynamically — every rule has a hard-coded recipient list, whereas Alertmanager routes based on alert labels, enabling automated escalation chains and team-based routing. The result: SAP teams experience higher alert fatigue because they cannot fine-tune conditions, and they cannot prevent alert storms from correlated failures.

Incident Response: FAANG incident response follows a structured lifecycle: Detection — Notification — Triage — Mitigation — Resolution — Postmortem. The Incident Commander (IC) role is a trained, non-technical coordinator who manages communication while engineers focus on mitigation. SAP incident response is typically less formal — the Basis team gets the page, investigates via SolMan, and escalates to the application team if needed. The lack of a structured IC role leads to communication overhead and slower mitigation.

# Prometheus alerting rule example
# File: /etc/prometheus/alerts/odata-service.yml

groups:
  - name: odata-service
    interval: 30s  # evaluation frequency
    rules:
      - alert: HighErrorRate
        expr: |
          rate(http_requests_total{service="odata-xyz", status=~"5.."}[5m])
          /
          rate(http_requests_total{service="odata-xyz"}[5m])
          > 0.05
        for: 5m  # sustain condition for 5 minutes
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: sap-sre
        annotations:
          summary: "OData XYZ error rate > 5%"
          description: >
            OData service {{ $labels.service }} error rate
            is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} for the last 5 minutes.
          runbook: "https://runbook/sap/high-error-rate"

      - alert: HighLatency
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.95,
            rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{
              service="odata-xyz"}[5m])) > 1.0
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: sap-sre
        annotations:
          summary: "OData XYZ p95 latency > 1s"
          description: >
            p95 latency is {{ $value }}s for 10 minutes.
          runbook: "https://runbook/sap/latency"

details: Staff+ Insight: Observability is the area where SAP engineers have the biggest learning curve — not because the concepts are hard, but because SAP's monitoring is fundamentally different. SAP monitors the SYSTEM (is the application server up? is the database connected?). FAANG monitors the SERVICE (is the user's request succeeding within acceptable latency?). The shift from system monitoring to service monitoring is conceptual, not technical. Once you understand it, everything else — PromQL queries, Grafana dashboards, distributed traces — becomes straightforward. Start by asking: 'What does a successful user interaction look like, and how do I measure it?' not 'What do I monitor?'
